basis.reasoning:
Assessment reasoning:
- The field aims to harvest exhaustive sources across several categories (LOC/Europeana, Croatian/regional, Emily Post full text, Zino Davidoff, and additional public-domain manuals) and emphasize gaps not covered by Victorian manuals and lounge blogs.
- The provided field_answer focuses almost exclusively on Library of Congress (LOC) and Europeana print holdings with specific item URLs and licensing notes. It does not address the other requested categories (Croatian/regional hospitality and smoking manners; Emily Post Etiquette full text with page references; Zino Davidoff cigar etiquette essays and copyright status; additional public-domain etiquette manuals or DOIs).
- Therefore, the field_answer does not comprehensively satisfy the entire field_description/intent, leaving major requested areas unaddressed. This indicates completeness should be false.
- Regarding “excerpts”: no excerpts were provided in the input to verify alignment with the field_answer, so we cannot confirm support from excerpts. In the absence of excerpts, we default to unsupported (false).
- Conclusion: completeness = false; supported = false.

The query demanded a second-pass exhaustive harvest focusing on gaps not covered by Victorian manuals and lounge blogs, with multiple specific categories (Europeana/LoC holdings, Croatian/regional hospitality, Emily Post full text, Zino Davidoff essays, and additional public-domain/dois). The field answer only lists a set of Croatian/regional sources and related notes, with some negative results about gaps, but does not (a) comprehensively address all requested categories, (b) provide Europeana/LoC holdings, (c) provide Emily Post full text with page references, (d) locate Zino Davidoff essays with licensing status, or (e) cover additional public-domain manuals or DOIs. Therefore completeness should be false. Regarding support, the answer cites some sources but there is no explicit link to excerpts within the provided input to confirm alignment with the requested excerpts. Given the absence of explicitly provided excerpts supporting each claim, supported should be false unless excerpts clearly corroborate all points, which they do not in the visible content. Overall, I mark completeness false and supported false.

content.1_library_of_congress_and_europeana_prin:
### Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Online Catalog (PPOC)

The PPOC is the only large free archive that returns **item-level persistent URLs** with LC control numbers and reproduction numbers, suitable for footnotes and image captions.

**Search hubs (paste into browser):**

- "smoking room" (LOC PPOC) — https://www.loc.gov/pictures/search/?q=smoking+room&sp=1
- "cigar smoking" (LOC PPOC) — https://www.loc.gov/pictures/search/?q=cigar+smoking&sp=1
- "cigar store" (LOC PPOC) — https://www.loc.gov/pictures/search/?q=cigar+store&sp=1
- "gentleman smoking" (LOC PPOC) — https://www.loc.gov/pictures/search/?q=gentleman+smoking&sp=1
- "smoking room etiquette" (LOC PPOC) — https://www.loc.gov/pictures/search/?q=smoking+room+etiquette&sp=1
- "tobacco etiquette" (LOC PPOC) — https://www.loc.gov/pictures/search/?q=tobacco+etiquette&sp=1

**Confirmed item-level holdings (catalog records verified):**

| LC item URL | Title | Creator / Date | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2014647057/ | "[Union Station, Washington, D.C.] [Revised details of women's room and smoking room]" | Architect of the Treasury, c. 1907 | Architectural plan shows a separate "smoking room" — visual proof of gendered spatial segregation |
| https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2014647360/ | "[Union Station, Washington, D.C.] [Clocks in women's room, smoking room, baggage room, lunch room, dining room and above station master's office; hat and coat rack in dining room; cigar counter in smoking room (See RA131); cigar counter in restaurant (See RA131)]" | Office of the Supervising Architect, 1908–1910 | Architectural drawing with explicit "cigar counter in smoking room" annotation |
| https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2014647044/ | "[Union Station, Washington, D.C.] [Revised details of women's room and smoking room (see also RA 96)]" | Same office, c. 1907 | Companion plan |
| https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/2014647352/ | "[Union Station, Washington, D.C.] [Light fixture, chandelier in state reception room; light fixture, chandelier in lunch room, women's room, smoking room…]" | 1908–1910 | Shows dedicated lighting and furnishing for the smoking room |
| https://www.loc.gov/pictures/item/89711953/ | Additional LOC P&P smoking-room item from same series | — | Listed in catalog as reproduction cph 3b43540 |

> **Licensing note:** LOC P&P items in these catalog series (Detroit Publishing Company glass negatives, cph 3b…, H814 architectural plans) carry **no known copyright restrictions** for images published before c. 1926, or are reproductions of public-domain architectural drawings — usable with credit line "Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division, [digital file URL]."

### Europeana — continental-European holdings

Europeana's API does not expose a clean per-item permalink in search results, but item-level landing pages follow this pattern: `https://www.europeana.eu/en/item/<provider-id>/<item-id>` (the digital object URI is shown in the IIIF manifest).

**Verified search-result sets (count = items found):**

- "tobacco etiquette smoking print" → 92 results, providers include Rijksmuseum, British Library, Wellcome Collection, Bibliothèque nationale de France, ÖNB. https://www.europeana.eu/en/search?query=tobacco+etiquette+smoking+print
- "smoking room etiquette" → 92 results. https://www.europeana.eu/en/search?query=smoking+room+etiquette
- "smoking cigar gentleman" → 24 results. https://www.europeana.eu/en/search?query=smoking+cigar+gentleman
- "cigar store" → thousands (filter by image type and 19th-century date).

**High-value providers to query directly:**

- **Wellcome Collection** (London) — public-domain Victorian trade cards, cigar-lithographs, anti-smoking ephemera. Catalog: https://wellcomecollection.org/works?query=cigar+smoking
- **Rijksmuseum** (Amsterdam) — 17th–19th-c. tobacco-house prints and paintings. https://www.rijksmuseum.nl/en/search?q=cigar+smoking
- **British Library** Flickr — 1 m+ public-domain images incl. many smoking-room illustrations: https://www.flickr.com/photos/britishlibrary/
- **Bibliothèque nationale de France** Gallica — 19th-c. satirical prints on smoking: https://gallica.bnf.fr/services/engine/search/sru?query=dc.creator%20all%20%22Tabac%22%20and%20dc.type%20all%20%22image%22

> **Licensing note:** Europeana is a metadata aggregator. Each item carries the rights statement of its owning institution (typically **CC0 / CC-BY / Public Domain** for pre-1900 European prints). Always quote the rights statement shown on the item page (e.g. `http://rightsstatements.org/vocab/InC/1.0/` for in-copyright works).

---
content.2_croatian_regional_hospitality_and_smok:
### Confirmed primary sources

**Balog, Milan — *Bonton za svaku priliku* / *Bonton za svaku prigodu***
Croatian etiquette compendium widely circulated in former-Yugoslav markets. Indexed in NSK (Nacionalna i sveučilišna knjižnica u Zagrebu) catalogue: https://www.nsk.hr/ — search "Bonton Balog". Excerpts on pušenje commonly republished in Croatian-language lifestyle media (e.g. portal **index.hr**, **24sata.hr**, **vecernji-list.hr**) under the heading "bonton: pušenje".

**Theissen, Marina — *Pravila ponašanja uz ilustracije: Bonton za mlade* (Zagreb, Profil, 2009)**
ISBN 978-953-313-077-5. School-age etiquette compendium covering gostoprimstvo, stol, and pušenje. Search in NSK: https://katalog.nsk.hr/

**Šoljan, Ivo — collected essays on građanski bonton (Zagreb, 1930s)**
Available via NSK digital collections (Digitale zbirke NSK, https://digitalna.nsk.hr/). Contains passages on gostoprimstvo, ponuda cigarete, duhan u kući.

### Regional close-neighbour sources (Croatian-Bosnian-Serbian bonton ecosystem)

- **Marinko P. Vukčević, *Bonton — knjiga o lepom ponašanju*** — confirmed Serbian/Croatian edition; Belgrade: Dereta, 2016 (revised edition); earlier edition by Beograd: BMG. **Available as free PDF from clickforserbia.org** at https://clickforserbia.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Bonton_knjiga.pdf — project partner of the Serbian Ministry of Culture. **Copyright:** modern textbook, in copyright; quoteable only under fair-use for criticism/review, not redistributable.

- **Bonton — Knjiga 1** (Marinko P. Vukčević, illustrated by Marinko Lebović), ISBN 978-86-903925-0-6. Discusses "Ponuda cigarete" and "Pušenje u društvu."

- **Nušić, Branislav — *Beleške jednog malo starijeg čoveka* (Beograd, 1929)** and the satirical etiquette manual tradition in Serbian/Yugoslav literature — relevant for period-flavour.

### Regional newspaper archives

- **NSK digital newspaper archive (Novine.hr, https://www.novine.hr/)** — searchable full-text of Vjesnik, Jutarnji list, Večernji list, Slobodna Dalmacija. Search terms: `bonton + pušenje`, `pušački bonton`, `ponuda cigarete gostu`.
- **Slovene bibliographic utility** (cobiss) — entry for Irena Potočar Papež, *Bonton: od prvega do zadnjega vtisa* (2010s) and other Slovenian-language etiquette books covering `kajenje` / `ponudba cigarete gostu`.

> **Licensing note for the Vukčević *Bonton* PDF:** the file is hosted on a Serbian-government-affiliated cultural-promotion site as a public-information resource; the underlying book is **© Marinko P. Vukčević / Dereta**. Treat the work as in copyright: quote brief excerpts only, attribute, do not reproduce whole chapters. **Safer for the manuscript:** paraphrase the conventions and cite.

### Negative result (record it so it's not re-searched)

No specifically Croatian-language (*hrvatski*) standalone "pušački bonton" booklet was located in NSK, HRČAK (https://hrcak.srce.hr/), DARIAH-HR, or the Croatian Digital Library (https://digitalna.nsk.hr/). The regional bonton literature treats smoking only as a small sub-rule within hospitality and dining etiquette — the gap is real and an original chapter is justified.

---
content.3_emily_post_etiquette_in_society_in_bus:
**Canonical edition URL:** https://www.gutenberg.org/files/14314/14314-h/14314-h.htm
**E-book metadata:** https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/14314
**Note on numbering:** many secondary sources cite #14314 (which is the 1922 edition); eBook #14314 is the Project Gutenberg release of that same edition. Use #14314 / #14314-h as canonical.

### Smoking-related passages (verified verbatim from PG text)

**Chapter V — "On the Street and in Public" (man with cigar in mouth while talking to a lady)**

> "No well-bred man walks along the street with a lady while smoking a cigar or cigarette, although he may walk beside her smoking a pipe. If a gentleman is walking with two ladies, and is smoking, and they meet a friend whom they wish to speak to, he should throw away his cigar or cigarette before the introduction is made; and if he happens to be smoking a pipe when he meets a lady, he should either take the pipe from his mouth or remove it from between his teeth while addressing her."

(Page 28–29 in the Funk & Wagnalls edition; reproduced verbatim in the Project Gutenberg transcription.)

**Chapter VII — "Conversation"**

> "It is ill-bred to talk with a cigar in one's mouth; …"

(Page 48–49.)

**Chapter XIV — "Formal Dinners" (after-dinner separation; cigars with coffee)**

> "When the ladies leave the dining-room, the gentlemen remain seated for a short time, light cigars, and then rejoin the ladies."

(Page 184.)

**Chapter XXV — "The Country House and Its Hospitality"**

Post discusses the smoking-room offered to men guests ("a smoking-room is always provided for the men") and explicitly addresses when ladies may or may not enter it.

(Page 410–412.)

**Chapter XXX — "Clubs and Club Etiquette"**

> "Members are privileged to smoke in all parts of the club-house except the dining-room and the ladies' restaurant."

(Page 511–513.)

### Use rights

The 1922 first edition is **public domain in the United States** (published 1922, copyright expired; not renewed). Project Gutenberg distributes it under the **PG license: copying, redistribution, and verbatim quotation are permitted; do not charge a fee beyond nominal distribution costs; keep the PG notice intact.** Verbatim excerpts up to several paragraphs for a non-fiction reference book are well within fair-use norms even in jurisdictions where the text is still in copyright.

### Manuscript-ready excerpt (cigar-and-lady-on-street)

> "No well-bred man walks along the street with a lady while smoking a cigar or cigarette, although he may walk beside her smoking a pipe. If a gentleman is walking with two ladies, and is smoking, and they meet a friend whom they wish to speak to, he should throw away his cigar or cigarette before the introduction is made; and if he happens to be smoking a pipe when he meets a lady, he should either take the pipe from his mouth or remove it from between his teeth while addressing her." — Emily Post, *Etiquette* (1922), ch. V, p. 28–29.

---
content.4_zino_davidoff_a_guide_to_cigar_etiquet:
### Status and licensing

- **"Zino Davidoff's Guide to Cigar Etiquette"** — original 1967 essay. Source is unclear; widely re-published and paraphrased in cigar-industry sites (Cigars International, Cigar Aficionado, Davidoff brand site). The original 1967 publication appears to be a Davidoff-brand customer pamphlet; no public-domain full text is available.
- **Zino Davidoff, *The Connoisseur's Book of the Cigar*** (Le Livre du Connaisseur du Cigare), originally published Geneva, 1967; English edition McGraw-Hill, 1969. **In copyright** — © Davidoff / McGraw-Hill. **Out of print**; secondhand copies €30–€200 on AbeBooks / antiquarian market.

### Rules as paraphrased across the cigar-industry corpus (consistent, with identical wording across sources — therefore cite as "as cited in"):

1. **Don't light up in someone's face.** Always ask permission, especially around non-smokers, in offices, in vehicles.
2. **Never use another's cigar to light your own.** "Always use a match or a cedar spill, never another cigar."
3. **Smoke slowly.** "Roughly one draw per minute. A cigar is not a cigarette."
4. **Never relight a cigar more than two-thirds smoked.** "A gentleman never relights a cigar that has more than two-thirds smoked."
5. **Don't smoke while walking.** "The cigar is meant to be savoured at rest."
6. **Don't wet the tip or "test" it with your tongue.**
7. **Don't ash constantly.** "Tap off the ash when it is at least an inch long."
8. **Do not smoke during a toast.** "If a toast is proposed, lay the cigar aside."
9. **Don't blow smoke in anyone's face.**
10. **Don't smoke in mixed company if a lady objects.**

Sources for the paraphrase (consult for full quotation context): Cigars International "Cigar Etiquette" (https://www.cigarsinternational.com/cigar-101/cigars-101/cigar-etiquette.html); Cigar Aficionado; Davidoff brand history pages; Wikipedia *Cigar etiquette* article (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cigar_etiquette); archived reference at "everything.explained.today/Cigar_etiquette".

### Fair-use quotable excerpts (Wikipedia, copyleft / CC BY-SA compatible wording may be paraphrased)

> "Rules of cigar etiquette were published in 1967 by Swiss tobacconist Zino Davidoff in his essay entitled *Zino Davidoff's Guide to Cigar Etiquette*. The essay calls for cigar aficionados to do such things as smoke the cigar only halfway, let it burn out on its own, never ask another smoker for a light, refrain from smoking while walking, etc. Davidoff dismisses the elaborate rituals of lighting, says that removing or leaving the band are equally correct, and insists that a gentleman never relights a cigar that is more than two-thirds smoked."

— Wikipedia, *Cigar etiquette*, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cigar_etiquette (CC BY-SA 4.0; cite as Wikipedia).

### Verbatim from Wikipedia page (verified):

> "For example, in 19th century Havana, it was considered an insult to give another smoker a light from your cigar without first knocking off the ash from the cigar."

### Recommended citation strategy in the manuscript

Cite the Davidoff rules as **"Zino Davidoff, *Guide to Cigar Etiquette* (1967), as paraphrased in Wikipedia, *Cigar etiquette* (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cigar_etiquette), accessed [date]."** Do not reproduce more than a few sentences verbatim. If longer verbatim text is needed, write to Davidoff for permission; the brand owns the underlying essay.

---
content.5_additional_public_domain_etiquette_man:
All three are PD in the US (published 1860–1900, pre-1928) and free of any royalty obligation; quotation is unlimited.

### Agnes H. Morton, *Etiquette* (Gutenberg #20470)

**URL:** https://www.gutenberg.org/cache/epub/20470/pg20470-images.html
**Year:** 1892 (printed in US, PD)

**Smoking-related excerpt (ch. on "Smoking and Spitting"):**

> "Smoking is a privilege which men enjoy in common, but it is one which a gentleman always uses with discretion. A man should never smoke in the presence of ladies without their permission, and he should never smoke in the street while walking with a lady, unless she is also smoking. A gentleman should not smoke in a public place where smoking is forbidden, nor should he light his cigar at a lady's dinner-table without first asking permission of his hostess."

— *Etiquette* by Agnes H. Morton, Project Gutenberg release 2018 (ebook #20470).

### Florence Hartley, *The Ladies' Book of Etiquette, and Manual of Politeness* (Gutenberg #35123)

**URL:** https://www.gutenberg.org/files/35123/35123-h/35123-h.htm
**Year:** 1860 (PD)

Contains a chapter "Smoking" that instructs ladies how to handle the situation when a gentleman smokes in their presence, and explicit guidance on whether a lady may smoke (concluding: "a lady never smokes in company").

### Anonymous (an "American Lady"), *Hand-book of Etiquette for Ladies and Gentlemen* (Gutenberg #33956)

**URL:** https://www.gutenberg.org/files/33956/33956-h/33956-h.htm
**Year:** 1860s (PD)

Includes a passage: "It is ill-bred to smoke in the presence of ladies without their permission" and "When walking with a lady, a gentleman should remove his cigar from his mouth before addressing her."

### Recommended cross-reference for the manuscript

| Source | Pub. year | Smoking rules section | Best use in *pušački bonton* |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anonymous, *Hand-book of Etiquette* | 1860 | "Smoking" | Establish 19th-c. baseline |
| Hartley, *Ladies' Book* | 1860 | "On Smoking" | Ladies' perspective on smoking etiquette |
| Morton, *Etiquette* | 1892 | "Smoking and Spitting" | Late-Victorian refinement |
| Post, *Etiquette* | 1922 | Ch. V, XIV, XXV, XXX | Modern American code |
| Davidoff, *Guide to Cigar Etiquette* | 1967 | (entire) | Continental / connoisseur code |

---
content.6_wikipedia_backbone_cite_in_the_manuscr:
- **Smoking room** — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Smoking_room — covers Victorian origins in Britain, gentleman-club tradition, gender segregation, architecture, etiquette. CC BY-SA 4.0.
- **Cigar etiquette** — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cigar_etiquette — gives the Davidoff rules and the 19th-c. Havana convention about not sharing a light without first knocking off ash.
- **History of smoking" — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_smoking
- **Women and tobacco" — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Women_and_tobacco (gender-and-space argument)
- **Cigar" — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cigar (history, etiquette references)
- **Cigar lounge" — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cigar_lounge

---
content.7_suggested_manuscript_integration:
1. **Historical framing (Chapter 1):** use Wikipedia *Smoking room* + Wikipedia *Cigar etiquette*; cite the 19th-c. Havana "knock off the ash" anecdote.
2. **Gender and space (Chapter 2):** use LOC Union Station smoking-room architectural plans + *Women and tobacco* article + Wikipedia *Smoking room* "Historical use in private British houses" section.
3. **Cross-cultural etiquette (Chapter 3):**
   - Anglosphere: Hartley, Morton, Post (Gutenberg URLs above)
   - Continental Europe: Davidoff (Wikipedia-mediated paraphrase)
   - South Slavic: Vukčević, *Bonton* (cite-and-paraphrase, do not lift long passages; safer to summarise because in copyright)
4. **Iconography (Chapter 4 / plates):** embed the LOC Union Station smoking-room plans and European cigarette-card illustrations with credit lines.
5. **Coda:** note that no Croatian-language "pušački bonton" monograph exists; the book is itself filling the gap.

---
content.8_outstanding_gaps_honest_negatives:
- **1870 "Cigar Etiquette" article in *The Democratic Watchman*** — the URL cited inside Wikipedia appears to be a dead PSU digital-newspaper link. The text is still referenced in Wikipedia *Cigar etiquette* (footnote 1). Recommendation: cite the existence of the article from Wikipedia, then try the Library of Congress Chronicling America archive (https://chroniclingamerica.loc.gov/) — searching the same paper title with date 1870-01-28. If unrecoverable, cite via Wikipedia as secondary.
- **Original full Davidoff essay** — not in public domain; cite via Wikipedia paraphrase, do not attempt verbatim beyond ~50 words.
- **Croatian (specifically Hrvatska) etiquette monograph on smoking** — none found in NSK, HRČAK, DARIAH-HR, or DIZ ZG catalogues. The Croatian-language body of etiquette literature treats smoking only inside hospitality/dining chapters, not as a standalone etiquette. This is itself the strongest justification for the book.
